is the first layer cool just kind of a nice dark lower mid-rangey sort of thing let's take a listen to layer two sort of a brighter area upper mid ranges sort of thing and then layer three very subtle hint of modulated noise to add some space and dimension to things once we put all three together what do we get lovely so first up one of the things i would like to instill in your mind when it comes to designing sounds in general is that often the most interesting things are something that is greater than the sum of its parts we're building up a sound from a couple of different layers and each of these layers is contributing some kind of specific aspect that complements the other parts of this whether this is designing a kick drum or a base or a pad or a lead whatever it may be usually there's something good to be done with layering in order to enhance the aspects of a sound you like or build on top of them rather than trying to make one sound that does it all more specific to pad design though is the idea of modulation and movement if i hold a note here for a sort of extended period think about what you're hearing and mind you i'm not touching anything i'm just holding some notes do you hear how the sound kind of grows and evolves and changes and never really is static that is a huge part of interesting sound design i think i mean there's sounds that work really well as static sounds maybe like an 808 being a prime example but when it comes to things like leads or some bases or pads especially we want this constant sense of evolution and movement and unpredictability because oftentimes the role of a pad is to pad out the arrangement we're going to have a very broad spectrum sound that's going to fill in a bunch of stuff and we're going to play long

pads especially we want this constant sense of evolution and movement and unpredictability because oftentimes the role of a pad is to pad out the arrangement we're going to have a very broad spectrum sound that's going to fill in a bunch of stuff and we're going to play long droning notes and it's often going to feed into big delays or washy reverbs or things like that and the thing with that being if it's too static it sounds boring and it just sounds like going on the whole time underneath the mix rather than something interesting and evolving that adds to the mood and the overall sonic palette and i guess mental imagery that your track is going to conjure up so modulation is really key for this kind of stuff more specifically to the synthesizer section here if we take a look at this first layer we'll see that we have wave table oscillators and i find that wave table synths are really kind of the go-to for pads at least for me a lot of the time wave sequencing synths are another really great thing as well because they provide a very similar benefit a lot of the times when we listen to older pad sounds you know your kind of classic detune saws or like a pulse mod square pad or something you know they sound a little basic and what the offerings of wave table synthesizers bring a lot of cool ideas to the table we have this constant movement and evolution we can sweep through the waveforms we can do lots of different things and with modern synthesizers we can blend lots and lots and lots of different things together and get these really really crazy results so i've got wave table here we're moving that around as well using these lfos we could blend in the second oscillator a bit we've got lfo3 targeting the filter cutoff of all three voices but i've got different filters which we'll talk about momentarily we've got the msegs as well i've used these to create some basic lfos just kind of a triangle lfo using that to target the global pitch of individual layers and that way we just get this constant moving flowing evolving thing when it comes to the individual layers here one of the big things i'm trying to do as mentioned is design complementary things that second layer so that these layers aren't really too similar there's different movements but similar things and that's an important concept this is a pretty restricted filter so it's 24 decibel per octave band pass we're moving this around and that's going to create this kind of mid-range to high-end layer and layering those together if we bring in our first layer underneath we've got something that feels like one sound it's very unified but it's two very different ideas that come together and form something that is bigger than the sum of its parts for a final bit of enhancement i usually like to add some kind of noise or something to my synth pad so if we go into layer three here this oscillator is actually entirely disabled but i have the slightest hint of noise i think only about decibel per octave fair amount of resonance not really anything going on with the filter envelope this is just being moved around from the lfo so this is coming from lfo3 all of these lfos as well a thing to note is that these are incredibly slow i mean a fraction of a fraction of a cycle per second so this noise almost imperceivable in the grand scheme of things but when we put it in it really
